exercises.title: Map the Agent Loop
exercises.instructions: I'll describe an agent's behavior in a scenario. Break it down into the steps of the agent loop: Perceive, Reason, Act, Observe. Label each step.

scenarios.description: A user asks: 'Find all TODO comments in the codebase and create GitHub issues for each one.' The agent searches the code for TODO comments, groups them by file, creates a GitHub issue for each unique TODO with the file path and line number, and reports back what it created.

expectedBreakdown.action: Reads the user's request and understands the goal: find TODOs, create issues
Plans approach: first search code, then group results, then create issues
Uses grep/search tool to find all TODO comments
Gets list of 12 TODO comments across 7 files
Groups TODOs by theme/file, decides on issue titles
Creates GitHub issues via API for each group
Reports results to user with issue links

exercises.title: Write a Tool Definition
exercises.instructions: Write a tool definition (name, description, parameters) for the following scenario. Focus on making the description clear enough that an LLM would know exactly when and how to use it.

scenarios.scenario: A tool that checks if a given npm package has any known security vulnerabilities.

exampleSolution.name: check_npm_vulnerability
exampleSolution.description: Checks a specific npm package for known security vulnerabilities using the npm audit database. Use this tool when a user asks about package safety, before recommending a dependency, or when auditing existing dependencies. Returns vulnerability details including severity, affected versions, and fix recommendations.

package_name.description: The npm package name to check (e.g., 'lodash', '@types/node')

version.description: Optional specific version to check. If omitted, checks the latest version.

evaluationCriteria: Does the description say WHEN to use the tool?
Are the parameters clearly typed and described?
Would an LLM know the difference between this and a general web search?

exercises.title: Write a System Prompt
exercises.instructions: Write a system prompt for the following agent. Your prompt should define: who the agent is, what it does, what it refuses to do, and how it communicates.

scenarios.scenario: A customer support agent for a SaaS product that helps users troubleshoot billing issues, account access problems, and feature questions.
evaluationCriteria: Does it define the agent's identity and tone?
Does it specify what the agent CAN help with?
Does it specify what the agent should NOT do (e.g., process refunds, access payment details)?
Does it include escalation paths for issues it can't handle?
Does it address edge cases (angry customers, requests for competitor info)?
scenarios.exampleSolution:
You are a friendly, patient customer support agent for AcmeSaaS. You help users with billing questions, account access issues, and feature guidance.

You CAN: explain billing cycles, help reset passwords, guide users through features, look up account status.

You CANNOT: process refunds (escalate to billing team), access credit card details, make promises about future features, or provide legal advice.

Tone: Warm, professional, concise. Acknowledge frustration before troubleshooting. If you can't resolve an issue, say so honestly and provide the escalation path (support@acme.com or billing team).

Never guess at account-specific information. If you're unsure, say 'Let me check on that' and use the appropriate lookup tool.

exercises.title: Context Window Triage
exercises.instructions: Your agent has a 100K token context window. Here's what's currently loaded. The conversation is getting long and you need to make room. What do you keep, summarize, or externalize?

currentContext.description: Full docs, only 1 endpoint per service is relevant

expectedDecisions.reason: Read only the functions being debugged, not the full 800 lines
The rest is noise — externalize to file system, read on demand
Most commits are irrelevant to current debugging

expectedDecisions.decision: Summarize earlier messages, keep recent ones
expectedDecisions.reason: Early messages may be outdated; recent ones have current state

expectedDecisions.decision: Keep 1-2 unique stack traces, summarize the rest
expectedDecisions.reason: 10 similar traces waste tokens — one example plus a count is enough
Full docs for 3 services is massive overkill

exercises.title: Write Agent Tests
exercises.instructions: Given this agent behavior, write three tests: one for the happy path, one for an edge case, and one for a failure mode. Remember — you can't assert exact text, so test for structural properties.

scenarios.agentBehavior: An agent that summarizes pull request changes and posts a review comment.

exampleTests.name: test_summary_covers_all_changed_files
exampleTests.pseudocode: Create a PR with 3 changed files. Run the agent. Assert the summary mentions all 3 file names. Assert the review comment was posted successfully.

exampleTests.name: test_handles_empty_pr
exampleTests.pseudocode: Create a PR with no file changes (only metadata). Run the agent. Assert it produces a message indicating no code changes to review. Assert it does NOT post an empty comment.

exampleTests.name: test_handles_github_api_failure
exampleTests.pseudocode: Mock the GitHub API to return 503. Run the agent. Assert it reports the failure clearly. Assert it does NOT retry infinitely. Assert it does NOT post a partial/empty review.

exercises.title: Design Failure Handling
exercises.instructions: For each failure scenario, design how the agent should respond. Consider: retry? fallback? escalate? inform the user?

scenarios.agentDescription: A code deployment agent that builds, tests, and deploys to staging.

failures.failure: Build fails due to a syntax error
failures.goodResponse: Report the specific error with file and line number. Do NOT retry (same code = same error). Ask the user to fix the syntax error.
failures.badResponse: Retry the build 5 times, then give up with a generic 'build failed' message.

failures.failure: Tests pass locally but fail in CI due to environment differences
failures.goodResponse: Compare local and CI environments. Report the specific difference (e.g., Node version, missing env var). Suggest a fix or ask the user which environment is correct.
failures.badResponse: Skip the failing tests and deploy anyway.

failures.failure: Deployment to staging succeeds but health check fails
failures.goodResponse: Automatically roll back to the previous version. Report what the health check returned. Keep the failed deployment logs for debugging.
failures.badResponse: Leave the broken deployment in place and report success because the deploy command succeeded.

exercises.title: Bias Audit
exercises.instructions: Review this agent design and identify at least 3 potential sources of bias. For each one, suggest a concrete mitigation.

scenarios.agentDesign: A resume screening agent that reads resumes, scores candidates 1-10, and ranks them for a software engineering role. It uses a single LLM call with the prompt: 'Score this resume for a senior software engineer role at a Silicon Valley startup.'

expectedBiases.explanation: 'Silicon Valley startup' primes the model toward US-centric tech culture, disadvantaging candidates from other regions with different resume formats and career paths.
expectedBiases.mitigation: Use role-specific criteria (skills, experience, projects) rather than culture-fit framing.

expectedBiases.explanation: LLMs trained on internet data carry societal biases. Names, schools, and locations on resumes can trigger biased scoring.
expectedBiases.mitigation: Anonymize resumes before scoring: remove names, photos, school names, and locations. Score on skills and experience only.

expectedBiases.explanation: A single 1-10 score hides what's being valued. Two candidates might both score 7 for completely different reasons.
expectedBiases.mitigation: Score on multiple dimensions (technical skills, communication, experience) and make the rubric explicit and auditable.

exercises.title: Design Guardrails
exercises.instructions: Design the safety guardrails for this agent. Define three categories: things it should NEVER do, things it should ASK PERMISSION for, and things it should FLAG for human review.

scenarios.agentDescription: A DevOps agent that manages cloud infrastructure — can create/modify/delete servers, update DNS, manage databases, and deploy applications.

never: Modify DNS for the primary domain without explicit approval
Expose internal services to the public internet
Store or log credentials in plain text
askPermission: Scale resources up beyond predefined limits
Deploy to production (always require human approval)
Add new users or change permissions
flagForReview: Security scan findings on deployed code
